posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked:
On the heels of a recent 2015 Bourgogne AC tasting TN: 1302 points worth of 2015 Bourgogne AC - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers, our group decided to redo the tasting with 2015 Vosne-Romanee AC as well.

Were all roughly opened at the same time, an hour or two before tasting. The only difference in treatment was the Fourrier, which had to be decanted into a separate bottle because of the wax capsule. I’m well aware this is not the correct format for enjoying these wines, and that on a given day, I’d happily put any single bottle on my dinner table and be thrilled. The (academic) interest and goal here was to see how these wines stack up with each other, and perhaps help inform purchasing decisions.

All in all, the wines are quite fleshy, and tend to show generous amounts of fruit. As far as the stereotypical Vosne spice is concerned, I can pick it up in some and not in others (and given the topic at hand, you can be quite certain I was looking for it). I think there is a dichotomy to be addressed as well with respect to how producers regard their village-level wines. Are they meant to be cellar defenders, or are they worthy wines in their own right, meant to be aged for a long time? With some producers (like Jadot or Drouhin), I felt that the goal was to make a wine that was easily accessible at youth, while other producers like Cathiard and CLF were looking to make wines for the longer haul. The different goals also make it more difficult to evaluate the wines side by side.

In the 2015 Bourgogne AC tasting, it was quite apparent that the generally-accepted best producers did end up on the top of the heap. Here, it was not as clear to me.

Note: for the convenience of the reader, the wines have been arranged in descending order from most favourite to least.

  • 2015 Domaine Bruno Clair Vosne-Romanée Les Champs Perdrix - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. Super light in colour – this is like a dark rose. Beautiful, just beautiful. So much pretty red fruit, with hints of red cherry, and most dominantly, strawberry juice. The palate is racy and mineral, with a juiciness and freshness that the other wines at the table couldn’t deliver today. Powerful, elegant, and complex. I love the freshness and crunchy fruit here. Delicious and exciting. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Jean-Marc Millot Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. One of the best wines of the lineup today. Some Asian five-spice powder and an intense red fruit note on the nose here. Clean, crisp, and absolutely entrancing. The palate is fleshy and easy, with a lot of ripe fruit and expressiveness on display here. On the other hand, the acidity and minerality here present enough to counterbalance that fruit. A very complete and enjoyable wine right now. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Joseph Drouhin Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. This has a very similar profile to the Clair. It’s a bright, red-fruited, and elegant wine, but the fruit veers a little further towards being candied. The palate is quite similar, but the tannins are coarse. It’s a less refined wine than the Clair, but still very good. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Louis Jadot Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. Light and delicious, with an elegance that I find absolutely entrancing. Perhaps not your prototypical Vosne-Romanée, given that its spice seems to be a touch attenuated, but this is absolutely drinkable, with its elegance and easy-going nature. It’s probably not a wine made for the longest haul, but the youthful accessibility here is lovely. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Bertrand et Axelle Machard de Gramont Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. A relatively new producer to the US, care of a generous newcomer to the group. This is a powerful and big wine, with lots of brown spice. Sappy and dense, with a palate that also comes across as a richer, fuller wine. Lovely intensity without heaviness in this wine, and the oak, while showing a little more toast, still is applied judiciously here. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Domaine Coquard Loison-Fleurot Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. Raw oak on the nose – lots of it – but fortunately it is held in check and nicely counterbalanced by the relatively high amount of stuffing here. There’s enough intensity and fruit on the palate that handles the oak properly. Somehow, despite all of this, there’s a lightness here (perhaps from the focus on red fruit and the bright acidity) that gives this wine lift. Very nice, if a touch modern. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Sylvain Cathiard Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. In contrast to Grivot, this seemed a little more in line with what I expected, with a touch more brown spice (though perhaps that might have been the oak poking through a little bit). Riper, fuller, and more expressive, with the finish showing a generous amount of tannin. Clearly an extracted and fleshier wine. Oddly enough, at various points of time, this showed an intense reductive stench that ebbed and flowed. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Domaine Georges Mugneret/Mugneret-Gibourg Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. Seems intensely purple on the nose. Lots and lots of fruit, both on the nose and palate. Doesn’t come across as particularly complex, and the palate seems relatively simple. There seems to be a light touch of oak here as well (or is that the Vosne spice?). Not entirely convincing. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Jean-Marie Fourrier Vosne-Romanée Aux Réas - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. There’s nothing exciting here, but this is still a very nice and approachable bottle of wine. On the denser and richer side of things, with a good amount of fruit concentration and a great dose of spice. On the other hand, this does come off a little simple on the palate. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Domaine Jean Grivot Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. This tends towards the crunchy side, with red fruit that is very light and fresh. Red cherries, a touch of cranberry, all good things to start out. The acidity is racy and light, but this feels just a tad tart. The finish comes off a little astringently too, which isn’t too pleasant. There’s a nice mineral cut here, but all in all, this feels just a touch simple. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Faiveley Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. A decent wine, if a bit non-descript. The palate mostly checks all the right boxes, but this also seems just a little bit closed down right now. Good fruit, a touch of spice, no overt oak, but alas, nothing here that really excites me either. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Forey Père et Fils Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. A weird tomato note on the nose in combination with a bit of reduction. There’s nice sweetness and fruit on the palate, but despite that, the finish doesn’t feel quite complete and you get a bit of tartness from the acidity. Lots of tannin in this somewhat gangly, angular showing. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Benjamin Leroux Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. High-toned, with a bit of volatility and those chemical solvent notes that evoke Sharpie markers. The palate is certainly nicer than the nose, but at the end of the day, the palate has a nice touch of red fruit and not much else. I joked that this was a ringer in that this was a Bourgogne AC. Too simple for the level. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Domaine Berthaut-Gerbet Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. Not the most appealing nose, with some reduced burnt rubber and oddly enough, some orange peel. Behind that are some pleasant light red fruit notes, with a touch of strawberry. The palate ends with some bitterness. On the palate, this seemed to be a little weaker and not as filled out as many of the other wines. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. Lots of oak on the nose and palate, to the point of bitterness. Clearly not my cup of tea, but there’s decent fruit to be found here, if you can get through the oak. The finish is hot and alcoholic. Not good. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Domaine Gille Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. The clear runt of the litter. Nailpolish and chemically nose. Weak and watery, with astringency and acidity. Huge pass. (93 pts.)
  • 2015 Emmanuel Rouget Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    Served single blind in a flight of 2015 Vosne-Romanée AC. Reduced and muted on the nose, though it’s almost certain this is a big wine. Muted on the palate as well, only showing some acid and alcohol. There was some debate as to whether this was corked – the consensus originally was no, but after a few hours (and the reveal and the following shock), some wet dishrag started to show up. (93- pts.)

link_counts.title: TN: 1302 points worth of 2015 Bourgogne AC - Wine Berserkers - international wine social media, online community, and discussion

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked: So you’re saying I shouldn’t have skipped over an offering on 15 Bruno Clair?!?

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked:
c fu:

So you’re saying I shouldn’t have skipped over an offering on 15 Bruno Clair?!?

I can only speak to the one wine we tasted but it was really damn good.

posts.name: p@ulbortin
posts.cooked: Some of these sound delicious thanks for the notes. Have never associated Clair with Vosne before.

posts.name: Marcus Dean
posts.cooked: Ben Leroux was my only dog in the race, it is like a dagger to my heart

posts.name: Sanjay Nandurkar
posts.cooked:
I have tasted only two wines from your line up:
Jean Grivot: amazing wine. Loved the perfume and spice. Classy stuff.

Amelie Berthaut: Loved this one too. Although it appeared a bit closed and not as showy.

posts.name: Subu Ramachandran
posts.cooked:
Great tasting, thanks for sharing the notes. I tasted a few from your lineup

Grivot: I enjoyed it quite a bit more than you did. I found good depth for a village wine.
Coquard Loison-Fleurot: It showed too much oak and polish for me as well. Too modern, not my style, and didn’t scream of vosne.
Mugneret-Gibourg: I had the 2014 which was incredible. Bummed that the 2015 didn’t show too well.
Meo-Camuzet: My notes concur with yours. Pass.
Jean-Marc Millot: Well doesn’t surprise me at all. Will take it any day. One of the best!

Need to try the Bruno Clair.

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked: Thanks for sharing!

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
c fu:

So you’re saying I shouldn’t have skipped over an offering on 15 Bruno Clair?!?

I was never a fun of Bruno Clair and its wines always remind in the shelves here in Quebec, Canada.

Quite surprised to read the TN…

posts.name: alan snitow
posts.cooked:
It is truly a bummer that so many village wines, most of those in this tasting are reasonable producers, start over $100.

Nothing folks dont know or which hasnt been hashed over before, but still a shame.

posts.name: R Nanda
posts.cooked:
I’m reasonably aligned with Adrian’s rank order, save the Meo which I liked quite a bit.

I found the wines to consistently represent Vosne though in two fairly different styles. One more forward, ripe and lush which plays to the solar '15 vintage (Cathiard, Meo, Colquard, Forey, etc) And a second lighter and more elegant (Clair, Drouhin, Millot). The second was a bit surprising to see in a line up of 2015s but goes to show that house style really does matter.

As for the Clair … it was really, really good. Most of us thought it was the Rouget. And it wasn’t just a bunch of yack palates that liked the Clair … several wine pros at the tasting including somms and master somms.

Like Adrian, wines listed in order of my favorites.

2015 Domaine Bruno Clair Vosne-Romanée Les Champs Perdrix - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. My WOTN and consensus group WOTN. Full, forward, intense nose of rich strawberry, a touch earthy and unending spice. The palate has excellent concentration yet is light and ethereal with fine tannin, making for a silk-like texture throughout. Pure AF. Spice crescendos and blows up in the finish. Impressive village wine. (94 pts.)

2015 Domaine Coquard Loison-Fleurot Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind – impressive value. Big sexy black cherry and that oozes Vosne spice and hint of oak spice as well. Rich dense and nearly creamy. Precise flavors of spice, black cherry and cocoa powder. Clearly some new oak influence here but the dense, rich black cherry carries it so well. Long and dense, with enough acid to carry and provide freshness. A producer that suits the Vosne well. (93 pts.)

2015 Bertrand et Axelle Machard de Gramont Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. A highlight and discovery of the tasting. Explosive nose has loads of Vosne spice with more red fruit than black cherry. The palate has good concentration with a suave, plush mouthfeel. Dense, but fresh. Impressive. (93 pts.)

2015 Joseph Drouhin Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. Light but not bothersome reduction as the ripe, sweet black cherry shines through with Vosne spice accenting throughout. Terrific lift to the concentrated, sexy red cherry. Intense herb and spice accents. Concentrated yet ethereal. All the flair of Vosne with the textural sensibility of Chambolle. Terrific. (93 pts.)

2015 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. Some reduction on the nose cloaks big, rich and non-delineated black cherry. Riper and richer on the palate, though delicious, with chewy tannin. Practically a barrel sample. Hugely concentrated and deep, with loads of spice and stuffing. Needs a decade+. Upside from here. (92 pts.)

2015 Sylvain Cathiard Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. Full, appealing nose of black cherry with intense spice and toasted brown sugar from the wood. The palate is delicious with forward black and red cherry, intense Vosne spice and a generous dollop of oaky. Flashy and hedonistic, though classic Vosne with the bones to age. Excellent village wine with upside from here. (92 pts.)

2015 Jean-Marc Millot Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. Lighter appearance. Aromas lead with a big Vosne spice and follow with lighter, ethereal red cherry, strawberry and rhubarb pie. The spice here is intense but not obvious. Generous and forward yet elegantly styled. Palate is similarly red fruited and sneaky spice. Fresh throughout. This will age on it’s acids. Excellent, with upside. (92 pts.)

2015 Forey Père et Fils Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. A touch of reduction clamps down rich ripe black cherry and plum that is laced with spice. The palate is fleshy and vibrantly fresh with an intense uptick of spice on the mid-palate. Fresh and lifted with increasing density in the finish. A bit clunky and not yet seamless, but loads of potential and upside. (92 pts.)

2015 Domaine Berthaut-Gerbet Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. Heavy reduction in the nose, with rich black fruit in the background that starts to get red fruited and spicy with swirling. Serious concentration of red fruit with richness and depth beyond a Village wine. Brooding and non-delineated with flashes of spice and layering with swirling. Upside from here in 5-10+ years. (90 pts.)

2015 Domaine Jean Grivot Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Singe blind. A more moderate and earthy nose with red cherry and subtle spice. Palate has moderate concentration and an elegant style. Very ready and easy to drink now, though a bit short in the finish. Red fruited with light spice throughout. More Chambolle than Vosne. A taste later in the evening had filled out – 90+ at that point. Upside from here. (89 pts.)

2015 Louis Jadot Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. Full nose has rich, sweet cherry fruit with well-integrated oak spice. Strikes me as almost new world in character. The palate is velvety and flush. The fruit is spice-filled and delicious, if a bit forward and simplistic at this stage. Better if this mellows and and builds some depth. (90 pts.)

2015 Domaine Georges Mugneret/Mugneret-Gibourg Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. Full nose of black cherry and spice is intense if a bit compact. Serious round, rich fruit and structure here. Underlying core of spice that is looking to emerge, but buried behind some wound up fruit. Not showing now, but clear stuffing that suggests upside. (89 pts.)

2015 Jean-Marie Fourrier Vosne-Romanée Aux Réas - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. Generous, forward nose that is rather simple with black cherry and spice. Palate is rich and dense, almost thick with spice-filled black fruit. Very 2015, by reputation. Long and tasty but inelegant. Fun now, but does this have the acidity to go the distance? (88 pts.)

2015 Faiveley Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. Bigger, non-delineated nose of abundant, rich black cherry with Vosne spice. The palate is immediately chunky with a cross section of black and red cherry that is impressively deep but clipped and abrupt with a firm finish. Needs a decade+. (88 pts.)

2015 Benjamin Leroux Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. High toned and very red fruited nose – one note aromas. The palate is very juicy and with fresh, charming red fruits. Simple with just a hint of spice. (87 pts.)

2015 Domaine Gille Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. Shy nose has bright red fruit and a hint of nail polish. Forward and red fruited on the palate but with mouth-puckering acidity. The acids complete crush the fruit making for a tough and awkward drink. Aside from the corked Rouget, this was clearly an outlier in an otherwise impressive line up.

2015 Emmanuel Rouget Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
Single blind. Muted and neutered throughout. No overt TCA, but this is mildly but certainly corked. NR (flawed)

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the notes guys.

The only wines I own of these are a few bottles of the Cathiard. But from your impressions I’m actually looking more forward to trying the Clair sometime!

posts.name: Clint S Q U I E R
posts.cooked: Thanks - I’m always partial to 93 pt wines
posts.display_username: Clint S Q U I E R

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
Peter Chiu:
c fu:

So you’re saying I shouldn’t have skipped over an offering on 15 Bruno Clair?!?

I was never a fun of Bruno Clair and its wines always remind in the shelves here in Quebec, Canada.

Quite surprised to read the TN…

Same. I haven’t been impressed. But I stopped buying/drinking them in the 2010 vintage.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: 2015 Mugneret Gibourg was great a few months ago. Sounds like it’s just shutting down.

posts.name: Vincent Fritzsche
posts.cooked: Nice line up. I visited Axelle Marchard de Gramont last summer in burgundy, we share an importer in Quebec and he recommended I visit. I was very impressed, and then happy to find the wines are available via Balanced Wine Selections. I loaded up last summer and then reloaded on Berserker Day. Check them out, no affiliation.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

2015 Mugneret Gibourg was great a few months ago. Sounds like it’s just shutting down.

I buy the M-G Vosne every time I can find it, and have yet to be disappointed (except by the price). Rich’s note and your comment make much more sense to me than Adrian’s note.

That being said, Adrian’s palate does not seem to align that well with mine.

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked: Cool exercise and good intel here. Also nice to get a couple opinions on the same wines. I’ve been looking at the CL-F wines and haven’t got around to sampling yet, though this will surely help me dive in.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: i don’t have much experience with it, but champs perdrix certainly appears like prime vosne real estate

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked:
m. ristev:

i don’t have much experience with it, but champs perdrix certainly appears like prime vosne real estate

Indeed, right next to a crappy plot of dirt they call “Les Gaudichots ou La Tâche”

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

2015 Mugneret Gibourg was great a few months ago. Sounds like it’s just shutting down.

Had this at the winery a year and a half ago and really liked it also. Too bad it did not show as well the night you guys had it.

posts.name: Subu Ramachandran
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

2015 Mugneret Gibourg was great a few months ago. Sounds like it’s just shutting down.

Good point William, never had a MG that was relatively simple.

With regard to CLF, try it before you buy it (wish I had). As long as you are ok with oak.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
A. So:
m. ristev:

i don’t have much experience with it, but champs perdrix certainly appears like prime vosne real estate

Indeed, right next to a crappy plot of dirt they call “Les Gaudichots ou La Tâche”

It’s definitely one of the over-performers in the range. And quite ethereal, from thin soils at the top of the slope.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Subu Ramachandran:
William Kelley:

2015 Mugneret Gibourg was great a few months ago. Sounds like it’s just shutting down.

Good point William, never had a MG that was relatively simple.

With regard to CLF, try it before you buy it (wish I had). As long as you are ok with oak.

It’s funny how tastings pan out sometimes. From barrel / tank samples in January 2017 the results would have been quite different, I suspect. And they will be different, again, and fifteen years.

From barrel, for example, the Cathiard was almost too much, but now it seems to have tightened up. I bought both it and Mugneret-Gibourg’s Vosne AOC, and would be amazed if the latter wasn’t in the lead at age fifteen. But it will be interesting to find out.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: just a heads up paris wine company is closing out their web shop with 30% off all orders over $300 + free shipping (coupon: merci30). they have the 15 de gramont vosne in stock and with the promotion it is about $55. sort of tempting but i have never tasted the producer so i will pass it on to those who have.

posts.name: R Nanda
posts.cooked:
Vincent Fritzsche:

Nice line up. I visited Axelle Marchard de Gramont last summer in burgundy, we share an importer in Quebec and he recommended I visit. I was very impressed, and then happy to find the wines are available via Balanced Wine Selections. I loaded up last summer and then reloaded on Berserker Day. Check them out, no affiliation.

Interesting, Vincent. I was super impressed too. Apparently they only (or mostly only) have Village-level holdings, and without the star power 1er and GC bottlings, the domaine flies below the radar. I am going to find and cellar the 2015 Vosne - it was special.

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:
A. So:
m. ristev:

i don’t have much experience with it, but champs perdrix certainly appears like prime vosne real estate

Indeed, right next to a crappy plot of dirt they call “Les Gaudichots ou La Tâche”

It’s definitely one of the over-performers in the range. And quite ethereal, from thin soils at the top of the slope.

Good information, thanks! I looked at the map and was shocked at the vineyard’s proximity to prime real estate. Of course, one has to discount the “across the road from La Tâche” as marketing sometimes.

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

It’s funny how tastings pan out sometimes. From barrel / tank samples in January 2017 the results would have been quite different, I suspect. And they will be different, again, and fifteen years.

From barrel, for example, the Cathiard was almost too much, but now it seems to have tightened up. I bought both it and Mugneret-Gibourg’s Vosne AOC, and would be amazed if the latter wasn’t in the lead at age fifteen. But it will be interesting to find out.

With big blind tastings like these, I’m always skeptical of the reproducibility of the results too. But I do like removing label bias from my evaluations. It helps me judge wines without any prejudices, and validates or refutes any impressions I may have formed of a producer.

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked:
Robert Pavlovich:

Cool exercise and good intel here. Also nice to get a couple opinions on the same wines. I’ve been looking at the CL-F wines and haven’t got around to sampling yet, though this will surely help me dive in.

To add to Subu’s point, I think the CLF wines are well-made, but they are modern in style. Not over the top, but they do show oak and polish. This one showed well in the blind lineup, but I also enjoyed a 2014 CdlR in a non blind lineup last year.

posts.name: CHIU LIN
posts.cooked: From what I have read, Bruno Clair Les Champs Perdrix may have also included some Petit Monts.

posts.name: Jo W Harbitz
posts.cooked: Great line up and great notes. Slightly confused about all getting 93 points though. Seems vintage beat producer in 2015.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
A. So:

Good information, thanks! I looked at the map and was shocked at the vineyard’s proximity to prime real estate. Of course, one has to discount the “across the road from La Tâche” as marketing sometimes.

Right… sometimes the grass really is greener on the other side!

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Had that Bruno Clair last week. Seriously good!

Thanks for the notes Adrian.

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked: Finally got a chance to look at the Coquard Loisson-Fleurot. Aromatics were way above what I expected. Found the palate to be rather lean for a 2015 red, it might be the freshest 15’ red I’ve sampled come to think. Still the fruit is ripe, if just. Didn’t catch any obtrusive oak, and it steered away from vanilla impressions which is always nice. Anyhow, if anything I could’ve done with more viscosity but came away fairly impressed and will probably look in to the GC’s at some point. From what I hear there is talent behind this label, improving as time goes on.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
I admit to having a large gap in my experience with Bruno Clair from the mid-to-late1990s vintages to post-2009, and limited experience, but it seems that there has been a change for the better. Can anyone elaborate? Maybe this has come up before?

A 2010 SLB Dominode we had a few months ago is very much in the style of your descriptions of this Vosne. Bright crunchy red/cherry fruit and real structural presence that grabs the midpalate. But a distinctly more transparent and less wood dominated style than I remember from the 1990s. I was surprised and impressed.

participants.name: Clint S Q U I E R
